content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
• 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Racquel Vasquez 
P.O. Box 380 
Lemon Grove, CA 91945 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-15-203 
Dear Ms. Vasquez: 
October 14, 2015 
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the gift provisions of the Political 
Reform Act (the "Act"). 1 
Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the 
Act and not under other general ethics rules such as agency incompatible activities prohibitions. 
FACTS 
You are a Councilmember and (this year) Mayor Pro Tern for the City of Lemon Grove. 
Your daughter is active at her high school, Helix Charter High School (a public school), and the 
Lemon Grove community. She is currently on the Speech and Debate Team, participates in sports, 
and enjoys competing in pageants with a focus on community service. She will also participate in a 
Debutant Ball next year. 
Fundraising activities (raffles, dining opportunities, the sale of advertisement, etc.) are 
required to sustain school programs. For example: 
• Your daughter was responsible for selling eight (8) tickets to a fundraising breakfast at $ I 0 
per ticket for a school team. You purchased the tickets. 
• Recently, your daughter was responsible for selling minimum $150 in advertisements for a 
pageant. You paid for the advertisement to meet the minimum requirement. However, the 
other participants were able to sell several tickets and secure many pages of advertisements 
through their parents' network of professional contacts. 
• Specific fundraising activities include the sale of tickets for raffles, tickets to attend 
breakfast fundraisers, and other opportunities. Also, advertising for placement in event 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


Our File No. A-15-203 
Page No. 2 
booklets and tickets to attend events. You stated that your official title will not be used to 
support any fundraising activities. 
Currently, you are not selling merchandise or tickets for school activities or sending letters 
requesting support to companies that do business in the City. Often parents are provided with a 
letter drafted by the school group or nonprofit organization which is distributed by the parents to 
their professional network. You are using your own money to help meet the minimum fundraising 
requirements. You would like to assist your daughter with fundraising opportunities just like every 
other parent rather than paying out of pocket which is costly. Your official title will not be used to 
support fundraising activities. 
QUESTION AND ANALYSIS 
Ca11 you help your da11ghte1· wit/, ftmdraisi11g to support lter !t·c/100/ a11d otlter activities 
wl,ere all of tlte ft111di11g will go directly to tlte scltool a11dlor designated orga11izatio11? Are there 
a11y regulations tltat require you to report the the ft111di11g as a11 elected official? 
J. General F1111draisi11g 
Your question implicates the gift rules of the Act. A "gift" is defined under Section 
82028(a) as ''any payment that confers a personal benefit on the recipient, to the extent that 
consideration of equal or greater value is not received and includes a rebate or discount in the price 
of anything of value unless the rebate or discount is made in the regular course of business to 
members of the public without regard to official status." In an effort to reduce improper influences 
on public officials, the Act regulates the receipt of gifts by local public officials in three ways: 
• 
First, the Act places limitations on the acceptance of gifts by certain public officials. The 
current limit is $460 from a single source in a calendar year. (Section 89503.) 
• 
Second, so that the public is made aware of any potential influences from gifts, the Act 
imposes reporting obligations on certain public officials, including Mayors, requiring that 
any gift (or any gifts that aggregate to $50 or more from the same source) received during 
the calendar year are disclosed on the officials' statements of economic interests. (Sections 
87200- 87210.) 
• 
Third, the Act prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using 
his or her position to influence the outcome of a governmental decision involving the donor 
of a gift or gifts with an aggregate value of $460 or more provided to, received by, or 
promised to the official within the 12 months prior to the date the decision is made. 
(Sections 87100 & 87103(e).) 
However, fundraising on behalf of a nonprofit organization or public school is not 
prohibited and does not implicate these provisions. 
Be/zested Payments. When elected officials fundraise for charitable purposes they may have 
to file behested payments reports. Note that Section 82015 of the Act requires elected officers to 


Our File No. A-15-203 
Page No. 3 
report payments made at their behest that would not otherwise be reported as contributions or gifts 
and are not made principally for a personal purpose. Section 82015(b)(2)(B)(iii) provides (in 
pertinent part) that payments solicited by elected officials for charitable purposes must be reported 
within 30 days if $5,000 or more is contributed (in the aggregate from the same source) in the same 
calendar year. The purpose of the "behested payment" reporting requirements is to capture 
reporting for payments that are not direct contributions or gifts to elected officials, but payments in 
which the public would have an interest, given the officials' role in the exchange. (Peak Advice 
Letter, No. A-12-094.) In this situation, however, it does not appear likely that you will be soliciting 
any payments for the school or nonprofit organization that would total $5,000 from a single donor. 
Therefore it is unlikely that behested payments reporting would apply. 
2. F1111draisi11g that be11ejits your daughter or that relieves you of a debt 
While you will not be accepting and keeping donations but rather conveying them to the 
school, under some circumstances such donations may still be considered a gift to you. In cases 
where a payment is made to and accepted and received by your daughter (or relieves a debt of you 
or your daughter2) Reb,ulation 18943(c) controls. Regulation 18943 describes circumstances when a 
gift to an official's family member may be considered a gift to the official. 
Regulation 18941 provides that a gift is both "received" and "accepted" when the official, or 
the official's family member, knowingly takes actual possession of the gift or is provided the 
benefit of the gift, or takes any action exercising direction or control over the gift. 3 In cases where a 
payment is made to and accepted and received by you or your daughter, Regulation I 8943(c) 
provides that the gifts will be considered gifts to you if either of the following applies: 
"(I) There is no established working, social, or similar relationship 
between the donor and the family member that would suggest an association 
between the donor and the family member suitable or appropriate for making the 
type of payment provided to the family member. 
"(2) There is evidence to suggest the donor had a purpose to influence the 
official:' 
1 Fundraising activity that might fall into this analysis might be fundraising required in order for your daughter 
to participate in a program, or cases where your daughter may be allocated benefils out of the donations she raises (such 
as funding her school trip). 
3 However, pursuant to Regulation I 8941 ( c ), a gift is neither accepted nor received if, within 30 days the gift is 
returned to the donor, donated to a 50 I ( c )(3) charitable organization or to a state, local, or federal government agency, 
or reimbursed to the donor. 
4 Evidence to suggest the donor had a purpose to influence the official exists in any of the 
following circumstances: (I) the payment is made to a family member of a state or local government 
agency official by a donor, or the donor's agent, if the donor is involved in an action or decision before the 
official's agency, in which the official will reasonably foreseeably participate or in an action or decision in 
which he or she has participated within the last 12 months; or (c) The payment is made lo a family member 
by a person who has a contract with the official's agency or by a person who engages in a business that 
regularly seeks contracts with or comes before the agency for the purpose of receiving a license, permit, or 
other entitlement and the official may reasonably foreseeably make or participate in a governmental 


Our File No. A-15-203 
Page No. 4 
The fundraising activity that you describe (raffles, dining opportunities, the sale of 
advertisement, etc., as required to sustain these programs) appear to fall under the general 
fundraising analysis at number 1 above and would not be prohibited or subject to reporting or 
potential conflicts of interest. 
Please note, since application of Regulation 18943 (part 2 of the discussion above) is very 
fact specific, you should contact us for further advice if such a situation arises. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
JWW:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Hyla P. Wagner 
eneral Counsel 
Ass stant General Counsel 
Leg I Division 
decision, as defined in the Act's conflict of interest regulations, related to the person, or has participated in 
any decision related to the person within 12 months of the time the gift is made.
